chunks: "That’s exactly right," he says, moving toward the couch. "Your parents called me this morning, worried sick about leaving you alone. They feared the temptation of the internet, of idle hands. But look at you. Reading the Word. Seeking redemption."  Upstairs, a floorboard creaks—Julian shifting his weight, perhaps, or Vivian adjusting her position to see through the slats. Pastor Jim’s head tilts up toward the sound, his brow furrowing for a fraction of a second. "The house settling," Maya says quickly, her heart hammering against her ribs so hard she fears the pastor will see the pulse in her throat. "It’s been doing that all morning. Ethan, show him your notes. From your devotional."  Ethan produces a notebook from the side table—his actual school notebook, but flipped to a page where he’s copied Psalm 119 in his careful, boyish handwriting. He hands it to the pastor, who settles onto the couch, his knees spreading, his Bible resting on his lap like a shield. "Beautiful," Pastor Jim murmurs, tracing the words with his finger. "Your penmanship has improved, Ethan. The Lord works through discipline."  Maya sits beside the pastor, close enough to smell the coffee on his breath, her hands folded in her lap with the sapphire ring prominently displayed, catching the light. She leans forward, her voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper. "We’ve been praying for you too, Pastor. For your guidance. We know we’ve... we’ve stumbled. But we want to be clean. We want to be worthy."  The words taste like copper and lies, but they flow like honey. Pastor Jim’s eyes soften, glistening with tears that threaten to spill. He reaches out and takes her hand, his thumb pressing against the ring, against the finger that Julian had sucked not twenty minutes prior. "You are worthy," he says, his voice thick. "You are fearfully and wonderfully made. The devil threw his worst at you—those vile images, those lies—and you are rising above.

Your parents will be so proud."  Behind them, in the closet above, a soft, rhythmic sound begins—barely audible, the wet friction of skin on skin, Julian’s hand between Vivian’s legs as they watch through the slats, her teeth sunk into his shoulder to muffle her breathing. The sound is drowned by the air conditioning, by the rustle of Pastor Jim’s polyester pants as he shifts, but Maya hears it in her bones, feels it in the heat pooling between her thighs. "We should pray," Ethan says, his voice higher than usual, his eyes darting toward the ceiling. He kneels on the carpet, his khakis pulling tight across his thighs, the outline of his erection still visible if one knew to look. "Before you go. A prayer of... of thanksgiving."
